title: Limitations of GDP
description: Practice what you've learned about the limitations of GDP in this exercise.
topic_path: Economic indicators and the business cycle

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an alternate measure of wellbeing currently used that incorporates GDP, but which does not rely exclusively on income as a measure of well-being?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Human Development Index (HDI)
Real GDP per capita
Real GDP per capita, adjusted for purchasing power parity (real GDP PPP)
National Income per capita
The Happy Planet Index

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements about real GDP per capita is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It doesn't account for population size
It doesn't account for pollution
It doesn't account for investments in future capacity
It doesn't account for inflation
It doesn't account for business inventory

assessment_items.question_content:
Hamsterville and Gerbilia are two neighboring countries with identical populations and identical GDP. 

**Which of the following would most likely make Hamsterville better off than Gerbilia even if neither country's GDP changes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An increase in life expectancy in Hamsterville but not Gerbilia
An increase in spending on healthcare in Hamsterville but not Gerbilia
An increase in spending on education in Hamsterville but not Gerbilia
An increase in exports from Gerbilia to Hamsterville
An increase in the pollution in Hamsterville but not Gerbilia

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains why GDP is an imperfect measure of wellbeing?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: GDP was never intended to measure wellbeing
It doesn't account for the fact that the same good might have different prices in different countries
GDP doesn't account for trade imbalances
GDP is constantly being revised
GDP is adjusted for differences in life expectancy

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes why we cannot always compare living standards between countries using real GDP per capita?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It might not represent living conditions of the typical household
It might overstate living standards if there are a lot of non-market transactions
It doesn't consider that there might be population differences between countries
It doesn't include government spending on social programs
It includes externalities like pollution

assessment_items.question_content:
Madinsonia has a real gross domestic production (GDP) per capita of $\$10{,}000$.

**Which of the following statements can we infer from this information?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Every person has an income of at least $\$10{,}000$
Every person has an income of exactly $\$10{,}000$
Every person has an income of less than $\$10{,}000$
The average income of a person is $\$10{,}000$, but this might not be the typical person's income
Without knowing the size of the population, we cannot say what a typical person earns

assessment_items.question_content:
A criticism of gross domestic product (GDP) is that it can both underestimate and overestimate production and well-being in an economy.

**Which of the following is a reason that GDP might underestimate the actual production and well-being in an economy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: GDP excludes production not traded in markets.
GDP is adjusted for changes in product quality and variety over time.
GDP ignores sustainability.
GDP ignores income distribution.
GDP includes how difficult production is for workers.

assessment_items.question_content:
The countries of Montrose and Bellaire have the same GDP. Each country only consumes two goods: fish tacos and air purifiers. It turns out that due to the way fish tacos are produced in Bellaire, there is a lot more air pollution in Bellaire than there is in Montrose. People in both countries like fish tacos, but really only put up with the air purifiers to clean up after making tacos.

Assume that people value fish tacos for the enjoyment of eating them. On the other hand, air purifiers don't bring anyone any enjoyment beyond cleaning up pollution.

The table below shows the national expenditures of each country:

Good | Montrose Spending (in Billions of $\$$)| Bellaire spending (in Billions of $\$$)
-|-
Fish Tacos | $\$100$ | $\$50$
Air purifiers | \$$50$ | $\$100$
GDP | $\$150$ | $\$150$

**Based on this information, what can we infer about the quality of life in these two countries?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Montrose has a better quality of life than Bellaire because they spend more on tacos
Bellaire has a better quality of life than Montrose because they spend more on air purifiers
They have the same quality of life because GDP is the same
They have the same quality of life because income distribution is the same
We cannot infer anything about the quality of life in these countries based on this information

assessment_items.question_content:
Hamsterville is a small, idyllic country where most of its citizens are farmers. Most of what the farmers produce is consumed by the farmers themselves. Hamsterville also has a thriving tourism industry in which cruise ship passengers visit and pay admission to hamster petting zoos. However, the income earned from the petting zoos is kept in the hands of very few people.

**Which of the following best describes why GDP might underestimate the amount of production that takes place in Hamsterville?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Much of what Hamsterville produces may involve non-marketed goods
Much of the income that Hamsterville earns is from tourism, which isn't counted in GDP
The distribution of the hamster zoo income is distributed very unequally in Hamsterville
There is no investment occurring in Hamsterville, so investment is missing from GDP
Farm production is never counted in GDP

assessment_items.question_content:
The expenditure categories of real GDP in 2017 for two countries  (Hamsterville and Gerbilalia) are given below:

Category | Spending by Hamsterville | Spending by Gerbilalia
-|-|-
C | $\$100$ | $\$100$
I | $\$100$ | $\$100$
G | $\$10$ | $\$10$
NX | $\$-10$ | $\$-10$
TOTAL | $\$150$|$\$150$

An economics student doing her dissertation on economic development in these two countries has noticed that the rate of capital depreciation in Hamsterville is twice as high as in Gerbilalia.

**Which of the following best describes a criticism of using GDP to compare these two countries that we can infer based on this information?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Both have trade deficits
Hamsterville will grow at a slower rate because more of its investment is replacing old capital
There is no criticism of GDP as a measure of output here because these two countries have identical GDP
One country has highly unequal distribution of income
One country likely has higher levels of pollution

assessment_items.question_content:
During the Prohibition era in the United States, the production and sale of alcoholic beverages was illegal. Despite this, widespread illegal production and sales of alcohol continued.

The 21st Amendment to the U.S. Consitution repealed this law in 1933, meaning that it was once again legal to produce and sell alcohol.

**Which of the following criticisms of GDP would explain why we might expect to see a sudden jump in real GDP in 1933?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: There might have also been an increase in inflation, which would distort GDP as a measure of production
GDP calculations only include goods that are produced and sold legally in markets
Alcohol sales benefitted only the alcohol smugglers and bootleggers
Alcohol should never have been made illegal
The pollution caused by alcohol production will increase

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is a limitation of gross domestic product (GDP) that causes GDP to understate production in an economy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: GDP doesn’t include nonmarket transactions.
GDP doesn’t include services.
GDP doesn’t include spending on the military.
GDP doesn’t count goods produced domestically but sold in other countries.
GDP doesn’t include goods that last a long time.
